name: Classroom Census Data Collection
description: Conduct a classroom census: collect data about students' favorite color, number of pets, hours of sleep last night, distance from school, or any other attribute. Create a list or table with student names and data. Discuss how you decided what to measure. Did anyone refuse to answer? Did anyone give a silly answer? What does that tell us? Graph the results and describe patterns: which color is most popular? How much do our sleep hours vary? Discuss: Why collect census data instead of asking a few students?
learning_objectives: Collect data using a systematic method
variations: Preschool/K: Use pictures instead of words (colors, favorite animals)
Middle School: Add structured survey with multiple questions and discuss response rate

name: Weather Data Logger Project
description: Over two weeks, record daily weather data: temperature (high and low), weather condition (sunny, cloudy, rainy), and one student observation (e.g., 'Did you need a jacket?'). Create a table with rows for dates and columns for each variable. At the end, create a graph showing temperature change over time. Calculate average temperature. Identify the warmest and coldest days. Look for patterns: did rainy days tend to be cooler? Did weekend weather differ from weekday weather? Discuss: how do weather services collect temperature data? Why measure it at a standard time each day?
variations: Foundation: Record just one variable (sunny or rainy) daily
High School: Compare recorded data to official meteorological data; discuss measurement discrepancies

name: Survey Design & Bias Detection
description: Design a survey to answer a question: 'What's the favorite recess activity?' or 'How much homework is too much?' Write 5-10 questions. Administer to classmates. Then discuss: Who answered? Who didn't? Did the wording of questions influence answers? (Test this: ask half the class 'Do you like homework?' and the other half 'Homework is important — agree or disagree?' Compare responses.) Were there options we missed? Create a chart of responses. Discuss bias: would the results be different if we surveyed a different group (younger kids, teachers, parents)? What bias might exist in our survey?
learning_objectives: Design a survey with clear, unbiased questions
Recognize how question wording influences answers
Identify potential sources of bias in surveys
variations: Elementary: Design simple yes/no questions
College: Add sampling strategy, confidence intervals, and hypothesis testing

name: Population vs. Sample Simulation
description: Fill a large jar with colored beads or balls (mixture of colors, e.g., 200 red, 100 blue, 50 green). This is the 'population.' Without opening the jar, students try to estimate the color distribution. Divide class into groups. Each group draws 20 beads without looking, records colors, and puts them back (sampling with replacement). Each group estimates the population distribution from their sample. Compare samples: do they all show the same colors? The same proportions? Why does the true population have 200 red, 100 blue, 50 green, but samples might show different proportions? Discuss: why sample instead of counting all beads? When is sampling the only option (e.g., polling voters, testing product quality)?
materials: colored beads or balls (or buttons, candies, etc.)
learning_objectives: Understand sampling as a method to estimate population characteristics
Compare sample results to known population
Understand when and why sampling is used
variations: Elementary: Use smaller jar, larger sample size for more stable results
High School: Calculate confidence interval from sample; discuss margin of error

name: Data Quality Audit
description: Given a data set (real or realistic), audit its quality. Look for: missing values (blanks), outliers (values that seem wrong), inconsistent entries (e.g., 'USA', 'US', 'united states' for the same country), typos, impossible values (age = 999, salary = 'N/A' where we need numbers). Create a report listing issues found. For each issue, discuss: is it a real problem or just unusual? How would we fix it? What decisions might be affected if we ignore these issues? Reflect: why is data quality important? Can 'dirty data' lead to wrong conclusions?
materials: data set (spreadsheet with deliberate quality issues)
tools to clean data if available
learning_objectives: Recognize how data quality affects analysis
Develop systematic approaches to data cleaning
variations: Elementary: Simpler data set with obvious errors
College: Use real data from open sources; teach data cleaning code

name: Mean vs. Median Explorer
description: Give students a data set: ages of students in a class (e.g., all 8th graders, all 9-10 years old, so 9,9,9,9,9,9,9,10,10,10). Calculate both mean and median. They should be similar. Now add one outlier: a 30-year-old substitute teacher is included in the 'class' data set. Recalculate mean and median. The mean jumps (from ~9.5 to ~12), but the median barely changes. Discuss: which measure better describes a typical student age? When would you use mean vs. median? Show another example where one is dramatically different from the other (e.g., salaries in a company where the CEO earns much more). Graph both data sets side by side.
materials: instructions for calculating mean and median
learning_objectives: Understand the difference between mean and median
Recognize when outliers affect mean more than median
Choose appropriate measures of center for different data sets
variations: Foundation: Use smaller data sets and very obvious outliers
High School: Add mode and calculate all three; discuss when to use each

name: Outlier Detective Challenge
description: Show a scatter plot or list of data values with one or more outliers: e.g., class test scores of 75, 78, 76, 72, 81, 77, 15 (one student scored 15). Challenge: identify the outlier(s). For each outlier, hypothesize why: Is it an error in recording? A student who didn't study? Someone who was sick? Is it a genuine rare event or a mistake? Discuss: should we remove outliers from analysis? Sometimes they're just errors; sometimes they're real and important. How would you investigate? What would happen to the mean, median, and range if we removed the 15? Emphasize: outliers require investigation, not automatic deletion.
learning_objectives: Understand when outliers are errors vs. genuine
Recognize impact of outliers on summaries
variations: Elementary: Very obvious outliers in small data sets
High School: Use box plots to formally identify outliers using 1.5 × IQR rule

name: Distribution Shape Investigation
description: Collect or analyze several different data sets and graph them as histograms or dot plots. Look for shapes: symmetric/normal (bell-shaped), skewed left (tail on left), skewed right (tail on right), bimodal (two peaks), uniform (flat). Examples: heights of students in a grade (probably roughly symmetric); test scores when most students do well but a few struggle (right-skewed); salaries in a company (right-skewed, with one CEO earning much more); number of siblings (possibly bimodal if some students are only children and others have many siblings). For each distribution, discuss: what does the shape tell us? Why might it have this shape? How does shape affect whether mean or median is better?
learning_objectives: Relate distribution shape to real-world context
Use shape to choose appropriate summaries
variations: Elementary: Compare just two simple distributions
College: Fit normal distributions and calculate standardized scores

name: Build a Dashboard from Survey Data
description: Conduct a class survey about favorite foods, hobbies, or reading preferences (or use existing survey data). Create a dashboard showing the results: multiple graphs on one page. Include: a bar chart (favorite food), a pie chart (reading preference), a line graph if applicable (how preferences vary by grade). Arrange graphs logically on the page. Add title and brief legend. Make design choices: what colors communicate the data effectively? Is the layout easy to follow? Share the dashboard and get feedback: does someone seeing it for the first time understand the results? Redesign based on feedback.
materials: graphing tools (spreadsheet, Tableau, or online visualization tool)
learning_objectives: Choose appropriate graph types for different variables
Combine multiple visualizations in one dashboard
Apply design principles to data visualization
Get and incorporate feedback on visual design
variations: Elementary: Single simple graph with title
High School: Add interactivity and drill-down capabilities

name: Misleading Graph Makeover
description: Show students a misleading graph (or a collection of them). Examples: a graph with y-axis starting at 50 instead of 0 (makes small changes look dramatic); a pie chart with 3D effects (distorts areas); dual axes with different scales (makes unrelated trends look correlated). Challenge: identify what's misleading. Explain the problem. Then redesign the graph to show the data honestly. What changed? How does the redesigned version communicate differently? Discuss: is the misleading version always intentional? Sometimes it's careless. Why does choice of starting point matter? How can responsible visualization help instead of deceive?
materials: redesign tools (paper and pencil, or software)
learning_objectives: Understand why visualization choices affect perception
variations: Elementary: Obvious misleading choices (missing labels, mislabeled axes)
College: Analyze why media outlets use misleading graphs

name: Coin Flip Probability Experiment
description: Flip a coin 10 times. Record heads vs. tails. Did you get exactly 5 and 5? Probably not. Flip 10 more times. Pool results with classmates. With 300 flips total, did you get closer to 50/50? Flip 100 more times (as a class). What proportion of flips were heads? Graph the cumulative proportion of heads as the number of flips increases. Does it settle toward 50%? This is the law of large numbers: randomness in small samples; consistency in large samples. Discuss: why would 100 flips give a more reliable estimate than 10? Does fairness mean exactly 50/50 in 10 flips?
learning_objectives: Understand the law of large numbers
variations: Foundation: Shorter flipping experiment; focus on unlikely vs. likely
High School: Calculate confidence intervals for true probability estimate

name: Taste Test Hypothesis Challenge
description: Formulate a hypothesis: 'Students can taste the difference between brand-name and generic cola.' Design a blind taste test. Have each student try two cups labeled A and B (one brand-name, one generic; order randomized). Ask: which tastes better? Or: can you identify which is which? Tally results. If it was pure guessing, how many would guess correctly by chance alone? (50% if two options.) Did more students guess correctly than chance would predict? Compute the percentage. Did students reliably distinguish? Discuss: why does the number of students matter? Is 7 out of 10 different from 70 out of 100? This is the beginning of hypothesis testing.
materials: two colas (brand and generic, or equivalent)
learning_objectives: Analyze results and compare to chance
Understand the basis of hypothesis testing
variations: Elementary: Simpler comparison (prefer A or B)
High School: Teach formal hypothesis test and p-value

name: Privacy Audit of Your Apps
description: Choose an app or website you use (social media, gaming, messaging). Read its privacy policy (or summary). Identify: what data does it collect? (Location? Messages? Photos? How long is it stored?) Who can access this data? (Company employees? Advertisers? Law enforcement?) What can the company do with your data? (Sell to advertisers? Use for recommendations?) Is your use of the app actually voluntary if you don't accept these terms? Create a report: what data does this app collect, and is that reasonable? Would you change your behavior knowing how your data is used? Discuss: is collecting location data without asking consent ever ethical? Should there be laws limiting what apps can collect?
materials: comparison table of policies from similar apps
learning_objectives: Consider privacy trade-offs in app use
variations: Middle School: Simpler privacy policies; focus on what data is collected
College: Add legal framework and regulatory context

name: Algorithmic Bias Case Study
description: Study a real-world case of algorithmic bias: facial recognition accuracy varies by race; hiring algorithms discriminate against certain groups; predictive policing targets specific neighborhoods. Read background information. Identify: how did the bias enter the algorithm? (Training data was biased? The algorithm optimized for the wrong metric? Feedback loops reinforced bias?) Who was harmed? What should have been done differently? Discuss: can algorithms be 'fair'? What does fairness mean? Should biased algorithms be used even if they're more 'efficient' than human judgment? Write a position statement: if you were in charge, how would you address this bias?
materials: case study (ProPublica COMPAS algorithm, Google Photos, or Amazon hiring algorithm)
learning_objectives: Understand impact of algorithmic bias on individuals and groups
variations: Middle School: Simpler example, focus on recognizing bias
College: Deep technical dive into fairness metrics and trade-offs

name: Data Ownership Scenario Analysis
description: Present scenarios about data ownership: A fitness app collects your heart rate, steps, and sleep. Does *it* own this data about *your* body? A company conducts a survey; does it own the responses? Genetic testing companies store DNA data; who owns your genetic information? A hospital combines patient data for research; is that ethical without asking each patient? For each scenario, discuss: who should own the data? Who benefits from it? What could go wrong if it's misused? What rules or laws might be needed? Debate: should companies be allowed to sell anonymized data they collect?
learning_objectives: Recognize tensions between different parties' interests
variations: Elementary: Simpler ownership questions (who owns class data?)
College: Add intellectual property and contractual frameworks
